name: Capers

book_claims.claim: One of the richest sources of the flavonoid quercetin with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties
book_claims.mechanism: Quercetin has shown to lower blood pressure and other metabolic syndrome factors in animal models. Quercetin and fisetin may kill abnormal senescent cells.

supplementary_research.finding: Comprehensive review found quercetin demonstrates significant anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer activities in both in vitro and in vivo models, supporting the book's claims about caper-derived quercetin.
Quercetin and fisetin identified as potent senolytics that selectively clear senescent cells in human adipose tissue explants and in mice, reducing senescent cell burden and extending healthspan.

Capers (Capparis spinosa) confirmed to contain the highest quercetin content of any common food, ranging from 180-328 mg per 100g, substantially exceeding onions, apples, and other quercetin-rich foods.
Quercetin supplementation shows reliable reductions in blood pressure (systolic -3 to -7 mmHg) and inflammatory markers (CRP) in hypertensive individuals. Senolytic effects confirmed at higher doses.

culinary_pairings.source: Classic piccata and Mediterranean salad dressings
Foundation of puttanesca and most caper-based sauces
Classic umami pairing in bagna cauda and pasta sauces
Salsa verde, tabbouleh variations, and garnishes
Foundational aromatic in nearly all caper-based preparations
Traditional garnish for grilled and baked fish across Mediterranean cuisines

seasonality: Caper bushes flower in summer (May-September in the Mediterranean). Fresh capers are pickled in brine or packed in salt for year-round use. Salt-packed capers generally considered superior in flavour.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble flavonoid. Bioavailability enhanced by consuming with olive oil or other fats. Half-life of ~11-25 hours allows accumulation with regular intake.
Converted to quercetin by gut bacteria. Slower absorption but more sustained plasma levels than free quercetin.
Another flavonol with anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties. Complementary to quercetin.
One of the highest polyphenol densities of any food. Includes diverse flavonoids, hydroxycinnamic acids, and tannins.

synergies.description: Combining quercetin-rich foods (capers, apples) with genistein-rich soybeans blocks prostate and ovarian cancer cell growth at lower doses than either alone
Fat from olive oil enhances absorption of quercetin and other fat-soluble flavonoids
